title: Making Pre-trained Language Models Great on Tabular Prediction (TP-BERTa)
authors: Jiahuan Yan, Bo Zheng, Hongxia Xu, Yiheng Zhu, Jian Wu (Zhejiang University); Danny Z. Chen (Univ. of Notre Dame); Jimeng Sun, Jintai Chen (UIUC)
tokenization_method: A RoBERTa-base LM adapted for tabular prediction via TWO bespoke modules. (A) Relative Magnitude Tokenization (RMT) for numerical values: (1) each numerical feature is discretized PER-FEATURE PER-DATASET by C4.5 target-aware binning (Kohavi & Sahami 1996) — recursively split the value range guided by the LABEL (equivalent to a 1-feature decision tree), boundaries of leaves become bins; max nbin=256 magnitude tokens. (2) These nbin bins are added as NEW WORDS to the RoBERTa vocabulary with randomly-initialized embeddings SHARED across all numerical features and datasets (a bin index = a relative magnitude). (3) The final numerical token embedding scales the bin-token embedding by the raw value: RMT(x) = E_extra[:,k] * x — so two values in the same bin still differ by a scalar multiple. (4) A magnitude-aware TRIPLET LOSS L_reg regularizes magnitude embeddings so nearby bins are closer in embedding space than distant bins (pull bin k1 toward nearby k2, push from far k3, with margin scaled by |k1-k3|-|k1-k2| over nbin), used only in pretraining (lambda=0.1). (B) Intra-Feature Attention (IFA): a SINGLE multi-head self-attention block, SHARED across all features and datasets, that fuses each feature's NAME token sequence and its VALUE token(s) into ONE vector before they ever reach the LM. For feature i, prepend a [CLS] embedding to the concat of name embeddings (l1 tokens) + value embeddings (l2 tokens; l2=1 for numerical), run MHSA, and take the [CLS] output as the single fused feature vector h_i. Position ids: [CLS]=0, magnitude/value token=0, feature-name tokens=1..l1; position encoding is added to Q/K (preserving name word order) but NOT to V (to protect randomly-initialized magnitude embeddings from a constant-position signal). The whole sample is then [CLS] (+) h_1 (+) ... (+) h_n fed to the RoBERTa encoder WITHOUT further position encoding (order-agnostic over features), and prediction is read off the encoder's [CLS] via a task-specific head. Categoricals/strings are mapped to meaningful text (value '0' of 'gender' -> 'male') and embedded by the normal LM word path.
feature_types_handled: numerical, categorical, binary, and free-text/string — unified in one scheme. Numerical handled by RMT (discrete magnitude token scaled by value); categorical/binary by mapping codes to meaningful strings then sub-word embedding (NO distinction made between binary and categorical); string features (e.g. 'movie comment') embedded the same way as feature names. Multi-class targets are EXCLUDED by design (only binary classification + regression). Missing values are not given a dedicated mechanism in the paper. No native time-axis / time-varying feature support — rows are unordered feature sets (the IFA + no-position design is explicitly order-agnostic).
token_granularity: Per-feature AFTER fusion: IFA collapses each feature's (name tokens + value token(s)) into exactly ONE vector h_i, so the LM-encoder sequence length equals the feature count + 1 ([CLS]). BEFORE fusion, granularity is mixed: a numerical value = 1 magnitude token; a categorical/text value = its sub-word token sequence; a feature name = its sub-word token sequence. So the IFA is the granularity-reducer: arbitrary-length name+value -> 1 token. This is the key contrast with full-template LM serialization (GReaT) and per-cell multi-token encoders (UniTabE/TransTab), whose sequences grow with cell text length.
numerical_strategy: TARGET-AWARE binning then magnitude-token embedding (RMT). C4.5 supervised discretization per numerical feature (uses train labels), up to 256 bins; bins become shared LM vocabulary tokens whose embeddings are learned and regularized by a magnitude-aware triplet loss to lie on an ordered manifold (t-SNE in Appendix F shows a regular 1-D-like manifold emerges after pretraining). The embedding is multiplied by the raw value to retain within-bin magnitude. This is explicitly positioned as superior to (a) Value2Str (digits as raw strings, GReaT) and (b) VMFE = value-multiplied feature-name embedding (FT-Transformer / TransTab / CT-BERT style). Ablation: substituting RMT with Value2Str costs -12.45% avg AUC, with VMFE costs -3.44% avg AUC (on significantly-changed datasets). nbin ablation: 128 -> -2.06%, 32 -> -3.59% (more bins generally better; a few tables prefer fewer).
categorical_strategy: Map raw category codes to MEANINGFUL natural-language strings (e.g. gender code 0 -> 'male'), then sub-word tokenize and embed through the standard RoBERTa word-embedding path; IFA fuses name+value into one vector. No integer-id lookup, no hashing, no target/CatBoost encoding for categoricals. Category SEMANTICS transfer for free across tables ('male/female' learned in pretraining transfers to 'boy/girl' at downstream) because they share the language embedding space — this is the headline transfer advantage over TransTab (needs overlapping column names) and XTab (dataset-specific featurizers).
high_cardinality_handling: No explicit high-cardinality mechanism and not studied. Because categorical VALUES are sub-word tokenized rather than integer-id'd, an unseen/rare category still composes a meaningful embedding from sub-word pieces (graceful fallback). But datasets with 'unmappable' categoricals (codes with no semantic string, e.g. job in {0,1,2}) were MANUALLY EXCLUDED from both pretraining and downstream — so the method's reported strength is conditioned on informative, low-to-moderate-cardinality, human-readable categories. High-cardinality string columns would also inflate the pre-IFA token count for that feature.
temporal_handling: None. The architecture is explicitly feature-order-agnostic (no position encoding across features); there is no patching, recurrence, channel-as-token, or time axis. Time-varying signals would have to be flattened to ordinary columns, losing temporal order.
scales_to_many_features: ARCHITECTURAL claim: post-IFA sequence length = number of features + 1, so each feature is one token regardless of name/value text length — this is the cheap, linear-in-feature-count property and is the genuine scaling contribution (the paper motivates IFA partly by 'computation burden caused by excessively long sequences when the feature amount is large'). EVIDENCE GAP for THIS topic: pretraining used ONLY datasets with 'no more than 32 features', and downstream datasets follow Grinsztajn et al. 2022's 'not high dimensional' regime — i.e. the 70+-feature setting is NOT evaluated anywhere in the paper. The RoBERTa encoder is still O(L^2) in L = (#features + 1), so 70+ features is admissible (well under the 512 token limit) but UNTESTED, and magnitude/feature embeddings were never trained at that width. IFA training-time ablation: removing IFA (feeding full templated text) raises avg training time to 1.32x and costs -4.17% avg AUC.
downstream_model: RoBERTa-base encoder (12-layer Transformer, HuggingFace Transformers 4.18) + a small shared IFA MHSA block + task-specific prediction heads (Dropout(Linear(Tanh(Linear(.))))). Read prediction off the encoder [CLS]. Finetuned with FIXED default hyperparameters (lr 1e-5, no weight decay, batch 64) on a single RTX 3090 — no per-dataset tuning, for time fairness. Also conceptually usable as a feature extractor but the paper uses it end-to-end.
pretraining_objective: SUPERVISED multi-dataset pretraining (NOT masked-LM / contrastive). Warp 101 binary-classification + 101 regression datasets (~10M samples total, drawn from the TabPertNet/CT-BERT semantic tabular database, each with >=10k rows and <=32 features) into one data-loader; each step samples a batch from one dataset and trains the SHARED TP-BERTa (RoBERTa + IFA) plus that dataset's OWN prediction head. Loss = supervised loss (BCE for classification, MSE for regression) + lambda * magnitude-aware triplet reg (lambda=0.1). Three released versions: classification-only, regression-only (these two = 'Ours_s'), and joint (Ours_j). 30 epochs, total batch 512, 4x A100; 72h (cls) / 98h (reg) / 143h (joint).
key_results: Evaluated as AVERAGE RANK over 80 downstream binary-classification + 65 regression datasets (<10k rows, medium-sized, low-dim). (1) Single-type pretrained 'Ours_s' with DEFAULT hyperparameters gets the BEST or near-best mean rank among all DNNs: cls All-rank 5.8 (best DNN; vs FTT(t) 6.8, SAINT(t) 8.2, MLP(t) 8.6, XTab(t) 9.8, TransTab(d) 11.0, TabNet(t) 12.1) and reg All-rank 4.3 (best overall, even ahead of tuned XGBoost 4.5 and CatBoost 5.5). (2) It is COMPETITIVE WITH but does not clearly beat tuned GBDTs overall; tuned XGBoost cls All 6.2 / reg 4.5, tuned CatBoost cls 5.9 / reg 5.5. (3) Feature-type dependence is the central finding: as categorical features dominate (larger alpha = #cat/#num, or beta = Shapley-sum ratio), TP-BERTa's rank improves sharply (cls alpha>=1 rank 4.4, beta>0.5 rank 4.5); when ALL features are numerical (alpha=0), it is INFERIOR (cls rank 7.5) and GBDT/FTT win. (4) Numerical-encoding ablation (Table 2): RMT beats Value2Str by +12.45% and VMFE by +3.44% avg AUC on significantly-changed datasets. (5) IFA ablation: -4.17% avg AUC and 1.32x training time without it. (6) Transfer (Table 3): tabular pretraining adds +3.16% avg AUC over random init and +2.79% over plain RoBERTa init on significantly-changed datasets (>3% gain on 26 / 21 datasets respectively). Joint (Ours_j) is consistently slightly WORSE than single-type (Ours_s).
baselines_compared: GBDTs: XGBoost, CatBoost (both default and Optuna-tuned). Deep tabular: MLP, TabNet, AutoInt, DCNv2, FT-Transformer (FTT), SAINT. Cross-table / pretrained: TransTab (cross-table), XTab (pretrained). Plus internal ablations vs Value2Str (GReaT-style raw-string numerics), VMFE (CT-BERT/FTT/TransTab-style value-times-name embedding), random-init and plain-RoBERTa-init TP-BERTa, and nbin/position-encoding variants.
transferable_idea: Two separable, cheap, ablation-validated tricks for tokenizing heterogeneous features into a Transformer/LM: (1) RELATIVE MAGNITUDE TOKENS — target-aware (C4.5) per-feature binning into a SHARED, ORDER-REGULARIZED token vocabulary (triplet loss enforces an ordered manifold), with the embedding scaled by the raw value to keep within-bin magnitude; this gives an LM a magnitude-aware, semantics-preserving numerical channel that DECOUPLES feature-name semantics from value magnitude (unlike value-times-name embeddings). (2) INTRA-FEATURE ATTENTION — a shared mini-self-attention that fuses each feature's name+value sub-tokens into exactly ONE token BEFORE the main encoder, making sequence length = feature count (not text length) and blocking cross-feature name-value contamination. Both are backbone-agnostic and directly implementable on top of any tokenizer for the 70+-feature setting; RMT in particular is the most reusable numerical-tokenization recipe here.
limitations: Authors-stated: (1) excels on categorical-dominated tables but is INFERIOR on purely numerical tables (precision loss inherent to LM/discretized representation) — GBDT/FTT preferred there. (2) Some tables favor a smaller nbin (over-representation with too many magnitude tokens). (3) Joint cls+regression pretraining underperforms single-type (incompatible task natures / dataset-specific-head strategy / base LM capacity). (4) Requires INFORMATIVE feature names and human-mappable categories — privacy-masked / semantically-incomplete / quantum-descriptor tables lose the LM advantage; such datasets were manually EXCLUDED. (5) Multi-class excluded by design. (6) LMs have a large hyperparameter space and longer training; mitigated by fixing default HPs. Reviewer-relevant gaps for THIS topic: 70+-feature and high-dimensional regimes never evaluated (pretrain <=32 features); no temporal axis; no missing-value mechanism; RMT binning needs LABELS (C4.5 is target-aware), so it is supervised per feature per dataset.
pitfalls: 1) RMT IS TARGET-AWARE (SUPERVISED) BINNING. C4.5 uses the training label to choose bin boundaries per numerical feature per dataset. This risks LABEL LEAKAGE if bins are fit on the full split or with too-fine granularity, and means the 'tokenization' is not unsupervised/transferable across tasks without refitting — borrow it only with strict train-only binning and validation. 2) PRETRAIN/DOWNSTREAM FEATURE-COUNT CEILING. Pretraining datasets are capped at <=32 features and downstream is 'not high dimensional'; the headline 'scales to many features' is an ARCHITECTURAL claim (1 token/feature), NOT an empirical result at 70+ features — magnitude and feature representations were never trained at that width, so transfer to 70+ heterogeneous features is unverified and may degrade. 3) WIN IS CATEGORICAL-DRIVEN. The clear lead over DNNs/GBDTs is on categorical-dominated tables; on all-numerical tables it LOSES. A 70+-feature numerical-heavy setting (the topic's stated mix is numerical + high-cardinality categorical) may not inherit the reported gains. 4) NEEDS HUMAN-READABLE SEMANTICS. Datasets with opaque feature names or unmappable category codes were excluded; high-cardinality categoricals with non-semantic ids (common in real wide tables) fall outside the demonstrated regime. 5) RANK-ONLY HEADLINE. Main results are AVERAGE RANKS, not absolute metrics, and 'competitive with GBDT' means comparable ranks, not dominance — tuned GBDTs still match or beat it on numerical and several datasets. 6) DEFAULT-HP COMPARISON ASYMMETRY. TP-BERTa uses fixed default HPs while baselines are Optuna-tuned 'for time fairness' — convenient but makes the comparison non-isolated (a tuned TP-BERTa is not reported).
relevance_to_topic: High on the mechanism axis, partial on the setting axis. It is a topic-native answer to 'how to tokenize numerical + categorical heterogeneity for an LM/transformer' with two reusable, cheap, ablation-validated modules (RMT target-aware magnitude tokens; IFA name-value fusion to 1 token/feature) that DECOUPLE name semantics from value magnitude and keep sequence length linear in feature count. BUT it was only validated at <=32 (pretrain) / low-dimensional (downstream) feature counts, has no temporal/missing handling, and wins mainly on categorical-dominated tables — so its direct numbers do NOT cover the 70+-mixed-feature target; the RMT and IFA ideas should be borrowed and re-validated at scale, not assumed to transfer.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/jyansir/tp-berta (official, PyTorch + HuggingFace Transformers 4.18). Pretrained checkpoints (cls-only, reg-only, joint) referenced. Pretraining data derives from the TabPertNet/CT-BERT semantic tabular database (~10M samples); reproducing pretraining needs ~4x A100 for 72-143h, but RMT + IFA are simple enough to reimplement on any backbone.
